content:
The leather of the binding cracked like old bone beneath my fingers. I sat at the heavy oak desk in the court wizard's chambers, the lamp burning low, casting long shadows across the spines of the journals my father had placed before me. Forty-seven volumes. Forty-seven predecessors. Forty-seven men of my blood who had stood at the right hand of the King of Thronegate and recorded what they witnessed. My father, his face lined with forty years of service, had not spoken as he laid them down. He had only looked at me with an expression I could not then name, but which I now recognize as the lingering echo of existential horror softened only by decades of acceptance.

My name is Kaedan. I am twenty-two years old. Seventy-two days ago, the Awakening ignited within my blood, and every day since, my strength, my speed, my magic, my very essence has multiplied a thousandfold. By the standards of any cosmos, I am a god. By the standards of the Thronegate court, I am an infant clutching at the hem of a being whose shadow casts shadows. I opened the first journal, and the dread began.

***

**Year 507,223 — Emrys — Charisma**

I cannot write this with steady hands. I have charmed kings. I have swayed the hearts of trillion-planet empires with a single oration. My charisma, amplified a thousandfold daily for thirty-one years, allows me to walk into any room on any world and command absolute devotion without uttering a syllable. I believed I understood the power of presence. I believed I understood what it meant to be loved. I understood nothing. I understood less than nothing.

They called themselves the Congregation of Unbound Wills. They were not an army. They were not a coalition. They were a philosophical impossibility given form: seven hundred trillion entities, each one a sovereign consciousness that had evolved across millions of years to possess absolute, irrevocable immunity to all forms of mental manipulation, emotional coercion, magical compulsion, and conceptual subjugation. They had purged themselves of the very capacity to be influenced. Their wills were locked. Their desires were their own. They had forged themselves into the ultimate weapon against any being who relied on dominance of mind or spirit.

They came to kill Godric because they believed his power lay in his physical supremacy and his arcane omnipotence. They believed that a being of pure will, incapable of being controlled, could not be touched by a tyrant who relied on the subjugation of others.

I stood beside Lord Godric on the observation platform when they arrived. Seven hundred trillion lights, each one a consciousness so fiercely independent that their mere proximity caused lesser minds to rebel against their own thoughts. They filled the sky with their defiance. They announced their purpose with a voice that shook the foundations of reality: they had come to free the omniverse from the tyrant king.

Godric did not respond. He did not raise his voice. He did not project an aura of dominance. He did not activate any power or channel any energy. He simply stood there, his ruggedly handsome face wearing the same warm, friendly expression he always wore when greeting visitors. His absolute concealment held firm. He projected nothing.

But his charisma stat had been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for over five hundred thousand years. Even at effectively zero percent of its true magnitude, even suppressed to a fraction so infinitesimal that it could not be measured, the passive weight of his charisma saturated the space around him like the gravitational field of a hyper-massive singularity that had been growing since the birth of time.

The Congregation felt it before they understood what they were feeling. The seven hundred trillion lights in the sky began to flicker. Their unified voice of defiance fractured into confused murmurs. They tried to marshal their wills, to lock their thoughts into the ironclad certainty of their mission. But Godric's charisma was not a compulsion. It was not domination. It was something far more horrifying: it was the absolute, inescapable reality of his presence. His charisma, even at zero percent, carried such overwhelming weight that being near him redefined the fundamental desires of any sentient entity.

They did not fall under his control. They fell under his appeal.

The first to break was a vast, crystalline intelligence that had existed since the birth of its multiverse. It had calculated every possible outcome of this confrontation. It had prepared itself for every form of coercion. It had not prepared itself for the simple, devastating experience of finding Lord Godric genuinely, deeply appealing. The crystal shattered from within, its fragments weeping tears of joy, as it prostrated itself before the observation platform. It did not moan or chant. It simply looked at Godric and found, for the first time in its billion-year existence, that it wanted nothing more than to be near him.

The cascade began. Seven hundred trillion entities, each one a fortress of unbreakable will, discovered that their wills had never been tested against something like this. They had prepared against domination. They had not prepared against devotion. Godric's charisma did not override their desires; it became their desire. The distinction is crucial, and it is what makes what happened next so soul-crushingly horrific.

They began to compete for his attention.

The first ten thousand entities nearest to the platform lunged toward him, their forms contorting with desperate, ecstatic longing. They did not attack. They worshiped. They clawed over each other, their previously inviolable wills now entirely consumed by the need to be acknowledged by the being before them. Those who could not reach him turned on each other with a ferocity born of jealousy. I watched in stomach-turning disbelief as entities that had existed as pure thought for eons suddenly manifested physical forms solely for the purpose of tearing each other apart with bare hands, their fingers gouging eyes that had never existed before, their teeth sinking into throats that had only just materialized, all for the chance to be closer to Godric.

Blood rained from the sky. Not metaphorical blood. Actual, physical blood, spraying from the bodies of entities that had never possessed bodies until their desperate need to worship gave them flesh, and that same flesh was now being ripped apart by billions of equally desperate competitors. The sky became a churning ocean of gore and adoration. Entities dissolved themselves into component particles, scattering their consciousness across the void, hoping that by becoming more diffuse they could encompass more of Godric's attention. Others compressed themselves into singularities of pure devotion, their physical forms collapsing under the weight of their own worship, crushing their organs into diamond-dense nodules of agonized love.

And Godric just stood there. He did not encourage them. He did not discourage them. He simply existed, and his existence was enough to unmake seven hundred trillion beings who had believed themselves beyond influence.

The collateral damage was total. The Congregation had brought their home realities with them—entire multiversal clusters anchored to their collective consciousness. As each entity destroyed itself in ecstatic worship, the reality it sustained collapsed. I watched decillions of universes fold in on themselves, their inhabitants never knowing that they were dying because their gods had looked upon a man and found him too beautiful to resist. Stars went supernova in sympathy. Galaxies imploded. Dimensional barriers shattered like sugar glass, dumping the contents of one reality into the next in a cascading nightmare of cosmic violence. The entire omniverse shuddered under the weight of seven hundred trillion simultaneous devotions.

When it was over, the sky was empty except for a fine, shimmering mist of dissolved consciousness and atomized blood. Seven hundred trillion beings had loved Lord Godric to death. Their love had killed them, and their love had killed everything they had ever touched.

Godric turned to me with that warm, friendly expression. "That was unfortunate," he said, his rich voice carrying a note of genuine sadness. "I had hoped they would simply leave. I do so dislike it when visitors hurt themselves."

I could not respond. My mouth would not form words. My own charisma, amplified a thousandfold daily, was as nothing compared to the passive aura of a being whose appeal had erased seven hundred trillion immortals without his conscious involvement. I looked at his kind, ruggedly handsome face, and I felt the same pull they had felt, and I hated myself for it, and I loved him for it, and the contradiction tore at my sanity like a blade.

He reached out and patted my shoulder. "Come, Emrys. Let us have dinner. You look exhausted."

I followed him. I will always follow him.

***

I closed Emrys's journal and sat in the suffocating silence of the chamber. My hands were shaking. I pressed them flat against the desk and forced myself to breathe. The date on the next volume was older—much older. I opened it.

***

**Year 89,441 — Torvald — Speed**

i am writing this in fragments because my hands will not stop shaking and my mind will not stop replaying what i saw and i cannot organize my thoughts because time itself has lost all meaning

lord godric asked me yesterday if i wanted to watch him test his speed i said yes because i am a fool and because i did not understand what speed meant to him

the enemy was called the Eternal Moment

it was a being that existed in the spaces between planck intervals it could move through time the way we move through space it could perceive every instant simultaneously and act in any of them it had conquered seventeen multiversal clusters by simply arriving at the moment of its enemies births and killing them before they drew their first breath

it was the fastest thing in existence

it was slower than nothing

lord godric stood in the courtyard and the eternal moment attacked it attacked him from every point in time simultaneously it struck him in the past present and future it delivered blows that landed before the decision to throw them

and lord godric

he turned his head

that was all he did he turned his head to look at a bird that had landed on the fountain

and in the time it took him to turn his head in the time it took for his neck muscles to contract by the smallest measurable fraction in the time between one planck second and the next

the entire omniverse ended and was reborn an infinite number of times

his speed stat has been multiplying infinitely every planck second for eighty nine thousand years i cannot calculate what that means because the number is not a number it is a violation of mathematics his neurons fire at speeds that make the eternal moment look like a frozen statue buried in ice at the bottom of a dead universe his thoughts process faster than time itself exists his body moves at velocities that render the concept of velocity meaningless

the eternal moment tried to strike him in the past and lord godric was already there he had always been there he had arrived at the moment of the eternal moments birth and had been present at its conception and had existed before its dimension was created not because he traveled there but because his speed is so far beyond the speed of time that the distinction between being somewhere and arriving somewhere no longer applies

the eternal moment did not die it was never born

lord godric moving at effectively zero percent of his true speed existing at a fraction of his actual velocity so small that zero is the only honest description had simply existed at a rate that unmade the eternal moment across all time simultaneously he did not chase it through time he outran time itself and in doing so rendered the beings existence impossible

but the speed the collateral speed the motion that bled off his merely turning his head

i saw it i cannot unsee it

the kinetic energy of his movement at zero percent capacity at a fraction of effort so small it does not qualify as effort caused the fabric of reality to ignite time itself caught fire the temporal dimension burned like paper every moment in history every past every future every present all happening at once as the shockwave of his speed propagated through every when and where simultaneously

decillions of multiverses were erased by the temporal fire their histories unmade their futures consumed their presents obliterated not by an attack but by the ambient velocity of a man turning his head

i watched a trillion timelines collapse into singularities of burning chronal energy i watched the birth of the omniverse and its death occur in the same instant i watched every moment that ever was or would be compressed into a single screaming point of impossible velocity

and then it stopped

lord godric had finished turning his head

he looked at the bird on the fountain and smiled

the omniverse was restored in zero seconds because he willed it so

the eternal moment was not restored it had never existed it could never exist because lord godrics speed had rendered the concept of its existence paradoxical

lord godric turned back to me his warm eyes crinkling with friendly concern torvald he said you look unwell shall i call for a healer

i could not answer because i had seen the end of time and the beginning of time and the space between them and in that space there was only him moving faster than existence could process and everything else was just debris in his wake

***

I set Torvald's journal aside. The pages were warped, as if the ink itself had been subjected to temporal distortion. Perhaps it had. I reached for the next volume with fingers that felt numb.

***

**Year 342,789 — Aldric — Luck**

Let me be precise. I am a man of calculation. I have spent my thirty-four years of amplified existence studying probability matrices and quantum outcome distributions. My intelligence multiplies a thousandfold daily. I can predict the behavior of galactic superclusters with 99.999% accuracy. I understand chance. I understand likelihood. I understand the mathematics of what might be.

What I witnessed in the Year 342,789 was not probability. It was the death of probability.

They called themselves the Calculated Certainty. They were not warriors. They were not gods. They were the architects of the single most statistically perfect assassination attempt in the history of existence. For twelve thousand years, they had calculated. Every variable. Every possibility. Every quantum fluctuation that might influence the outcome of their attack. They had mapped the probability space of the entire omniverse and identified a sequence of events that, according to every mathematical model, had a 99.97% chance of resulting in Lord Godric's destruction.

Their weapon was not a blade or a spell. Their weapon was a sequence. A carefully choreographed chain of quantum events, each one triggering the next, designed to culminate in a specific outcome: the cessation of Lord Godric's existence. They had accounted for his physical strength. They had accounted for his magical power. They had accounted for his omniscience. Their sequence operated through channels that should have been beyond his ability to influence: random quantum decay, chaotic atmospheric fluctuations, the spontaneous generation and annihilation of virtual particles. They had built their assassination into the fundamental randomness of reality itself.

They launched their sequence. I watched the probability matrices in real-time through my amplified perception. The sequence began flawlessly. Each quantum event triggered the next with perfect precision. The chain reaction propagated through reality like a flawless row of falling dominoes, each impact exactly as calculated.

And then, at step 7,447,832, a virtual particle that should have annihilated its antiparticle instead persisted for 10^-43 seconds longer than probability dictated.

That was all. A fraction of a fraction of a fraction of a moment. A deviation so small that it fell below the threshold of measurable uncertainty.

But Lord Godric's luck stat had been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for over three hundred thousand years. His luck was not good fortune. His luck was the absolute, sovereign dominion over probability itself. Even at effectively zero percent of its true magnitude, his luck bent reality around him like light bending around a black hole. The virtual particle that persisted should not have persisted. It persisted because Lord Godric existed, and his existence made impossible things happen.

The sequence diverged. Step 7,447,833 did not trigger as planned. Instead, a cascading failure began that the Calculated Certainty had deemed so astronomically unlikely that they had not prepared contingency plans for it. The failure propagated backward through the sequence, untriggering events that had already occurred, rewriting quantum states that had already collapsed.

I watched in mathematical horror as the probability of their success dropped from 99.97% to 0% in the span of three Planck seconds.

But the horror did not end with failure. Lord Godric's luck did not merely protect him. It punished those who had attempted to harm him. The same probabilistic distortion that had saved him now turned against the Calculated Certainty with vicious, comprehensive cruelty.

Every calculation they had made became wrong. Every variable they had mapped became unreliable. The quantum events they had relied upon began to invert. Random particles that should have decayed persisted. Stable configurations collapsed without cause. The mathematical foundations of their entire civilization began to hemorrhage consistency.

I watched as the laws of physics themselves betrayed the Calculated Certainty. Gravity reversed in their home dimension, crushing their worlds against the ceiling of space. Nuclear forces suddenly weakened, causing their stars to disintegrate into cold, expanding clouds of subatomic particles. Electromagnetic bonds dissolved, and the bodies of their trillion-strong population fell apart into clouds of unbound quarks, each one experiencing the agony of existence without structure.

The probabilistic collapse spread beyond their dimension. The same distortion that had destroyed the Calculated Certainty began to infect the surrounding realities. In decillions of multiverses, probability itself went mad. Stars spontaneously transformed into gold. Oceans reversed their flow and launched themselves into the sky. The fundamental constants of nature began to fluctuate wildly, causing physics to behave like a fever dream. Entire universes experienced every possible state of existence simultaneously, their inhabitants trapped in quantum superpositions of agony, unable to live or die, existing in a permanent state of probabilistic torture.

The omniverse itself convulsed under the weight of Lord Godric's luck. Reality became a gambling table, and every roll of the dice came up in his favor, and the house always wins, and the house was him.

When the distortion finally stabilized, the Calculated Certainty had been erased from existence. Their home dimension was a void of frozen probability, a space where nothing could happen because chance itself had died. Decillions of multiverses had been damaged or destroyed by the probabilistic cascade. The suffering was incalculable. The destruction was absolute.

Lord Godric had not moved. He had not spoken. He had not activated any power or expressed any will. He had simply existed, and his luck had done what luck always did: it had ensured that everything went his way, and it had destroyed everything that did not.

He looked at me with those warm, fatherly eyes. "Aldric," he said gently, "did you see that? The most remarkable thing happened. A bird landed on my windowsill, and I had hoped it would stay, and it did. Is that not pleasant?"

He had not noticed the destruction of decillions of multiverses caused by his passive luck. Or perhaps he had noticed and simply did not consider it significant. I do not know which possibility is more terrifying.

I nodded. I told him it was pleasant. I did not mention the screams of a trillion collapsing realities, because I did not think he would understand why they mattered.

***

I pressed my palms against my eyes until I saw stars. The journals were destroying me, and I had only read three. My thousandfold-amplified mind was struggling to process the scale of what I was learning. Each entry revealed the same horrifying truth from a different angle: Lord Godric was not merely powerful. He was the defining fact of existence, and everything else was debris in his wake.

I picked up the next journal. The leather was cracked and stained with something that might have been tears.

***

**Year 178,334 — Caius — Strength**

I am a soldier's son. I grew up understanding force. I learned to break bones before I learned to read. When the Awakening ignited in my blood, my strength multiplied a thousandfold every day, and I learned what it meant to be strong. I can shatter planets with my fists. I can crush stars between my fingers. I am the strongest mortal who has ever lived in the history of the Thronegate court.

I am an insect. I am a bacterium clinging to the hoof of a behemoth that strides through realities like wheat fields.

The being that came to challenge Lord Godric was called the World-Breaker. It was not a creature. It was a force of nature given consciousness. Its muscles contained the compressed mass of a googolplexian galaxies. Its punches generated kinetic energy sufficient to shatter the barriers between multiversal clusters. It had killed gods of war. It had destroyed empires of infinite fortitude. It believed, with the absolute certainty of its existence, that nothing in creation could match its strength.

It was wrong. It was so wrong that the concept of wrongness must be expanded to accommodate the magnitude of its error.

Lord Godric was sitting in his garden when the World-Breaker arrived. He was tending to a rose bush, his large, calloused hands carefully pruning dead blooms. He wore simple clothing and an expression of peaceful contentment. When the sky split open and the World-Breaker descended, its gravitational field warping the fabric of the garden itself, Lord Godric did not look up from his roses.

The World-Breaker threw a punch. The force of its swing erased decillions of multiverses as collateral wind. The kinetic pressure alone reduced surrounding galaxies to clouds of subatomic debris. The punch itself contained the strength of a googolplexian collapsing universes compressed into the area of a single fist. It was the hardest punch ever thrown in the history of existence.

Lord Godric continued pruning his rose.

The punch reached him, and Lord Godric's strength—his resting, passive, effectively-zero-percent strength—simply existed. He did not brace. He did not flex. He did not raise a hand or turn his body or activate any defense. His musculature, multiplying at rates beyond infinite recursion every Planck second for nearly two hundred thousand years, generated passive force that made the World-Breaker's attack look like a whisper in a hurricane.

The punch struck Lord Godric's shoulder and stopped. Not because of durability, though his durability was equally infinite. The punch stopped because of strength. Because the resting tension in Lord Godric's muscles generated more force than the World-Breaker could produce with every fiber of its being. The kinetic energy of the World-Breaker's attack was absorbed not by resistance but by overwhelming opposition. The force of Lord Godric's resting muscles pushed back against the punch with strength so disproportionate that the World-Breaker's arm disintegrated on contact.

The destruction traveled up the World-Breaker's limb like lightning through wet timber. The bones, which had never broken under any force, shattered into powder so fine it ceased to be matter. The muscles, which contained the mass of googolplexian galaxies, were torn apart by the sheer pressure differential between Lord Godric's resting strength and the World-Breaker's maximum output. The flesh peeled away in wet, screaming strips. The blood, superheated by the friction of forces beyond comprehension, vaporized into a cloud of crimson steam.

But the World-Breaker did not die quickly. Lord Godric's strength did not merely defeat the attack; it reflected it, amplified it, multiplied it by infinities. The shockwave of the reflected force propagated outward from the point of contact and consumed everything.

The garden was erased. The planet was erased. The galaxy was erased. Decillions of multiverses were erased. The entire omniverse was erased. Everything that existed was obliterated by the ambient expression of Lord Godric's resting strength encountering a force that dared to challenge it. The collateral damage was not intentional. It was simply the inevitable consequence of strength so vast that its passive presence could not coexist with opposition.

I floated in the void, protected by whatever mechanism Lord Godric employed to preserve those he wished preserved, and I watched the nothingness that had been everything.

Lord Godric sighed. "My roses," he said, with genuine disappointment.

In zero seconds, the omniverse was restored. Every reality, every dimension, every being—restored perfectly, as if the annihilation had never occurred.

Everything was restored except the World-Breaker. It remained as a spreading smear of pulverized matter across the restored garden stones, still alive, still conscious, still experiencing the infinite agony of being unmade by strength that had not even tried to destroy it.

Lord Godric looked at the smear with mild disapproval. Then he looked at me, and his expression softened into warmth. "Caius," he said, reaching out to steady my trembling body, "you are shaking. Come, let me make you tea. You must not let the excitement upset your constitution."

I drank the tea. It was excellent. I have never told anyone about the tears that fell into the cup.

***

One more journal. I could see the end of the stack. My hands were shaking so badly that I nearly knocked the final volume to the floor. I opened it with the desperate determination of a man who knows that the only way out is through.

***

**Year 722,891 — Fenric — Senses**

I have spent my life studying perception. My senses, amplified a thousandfold daily for twenty-six years, allow me to perceive the quantum foam underlying reality. I can hear the birth cries of stars in galaxies beyond the observable horizon. I can see the individual threads of spacetime weaving together to form the fabric of existence. I can taste the decay of concepts at the edge of the void. I believed my perception was extraordinary.

I believed wrong. I believed so wrong that the universe itself must have been laughing at my arrogance.

Lord Godric asked me to accompany him to the perimeter observation post. He said he was curious about something. He often says he is curious about something. I have learned that when Lord Godric is curious, existence suffers.

The entity that appeared was called the Unseen. It was not invisible. It was unperceivable. It existed in a state of quantum superposition across all possible dimensions simultaneously, its form never collapsing into an observable state. No instrument could detect it. No sense could perceive it. No magic could reveal it. It had slaughtered entire civilizations without ever being observed, because observation was impossible. It killed from a state of absolute sensory negation.

It came for Lord Godric. It came with absolute confidence that the King of Thronegate, for all his power, could not fight what he could not perceive.

Lord Godric stood at the observation window. He did not turn. He did not focus. He did not activate any sensory enhancement or channel any magical perception. He simply stood there, his eyes gazing out at the stars with the relaxed, unfocused expression of a man lost in pleasant thought.

But his senses had been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for over seven hundred thousand years. His perception, even at effectively zero percent of its true capacity, even suppressed to a fraction so small that zero was the only accurate description, operated at a level that made my amplified senses look like the dim awareness of an amoeba floating in a drop of pond water.

Lord Godric perceived the Unseen.

The horror of what happened next is not in the violence, though the violence was extreme. The horror is in the mechanism. The Unseen's power lay in its unperceivability. Its form existed in superposition because observation caused collapse, and it had evolved to resist observation from any source. But Lord Godric's perception was not a source. Lord Godric's perception was an absolute force. It was the categorical imperative of observation. When he perceived something, it was perceived. Not because his senses reached out to detect it, but because his senses defined the boundary between the perceived and the unperceived.

The Unseen collapsed. Not because Lord Godric forced it. Because his perception made its superposition impossible. It was like a shadow trying to exist in the face of a light that had been growing brighter for seven hundred thousand years. The shadow did not retreat. It was simply rendered impossible.

The collapse was catastrophic. The Unseen's form, forced into a single observable state for the first time in its existence, could not withstand the weight of being perceived. Its quantum structure detonated. Every particle that had existed in superposition now occupied a single position simultaneously, and the energy release was equivalent to the simultaneous detonation of a decillion universes.

The explosion took the form of a scream. Not a sound. A conceptual scream, a shriek of absolute agony as an entity that had never been observed suddenly experienced the crushing, unbearable weight of being seen by an omniscient god. The Unseen's body manifested as a towering column of eyes—billions of them, trillions, each one weeping blood and optic fluid as the sheer act of being perceived caused its anatomy to liquefy under the pressure of observation.

The sensory shockwave propagated outward. Lord Godric's perception, having collapsed the Unseen, continued to expand. His senses, even at zero percent, were so acute that the act of perceiving the Unseen's destruction caused a chain reaction of observation that collapsed quantum superpositions across the entire omniverse. Every particle in existence that had been in an uncertain state suddenly found itself observed. The wavefunction of the omniverse collapsed into a single, absolute state.

The collateral damage was incalculable. Reality itself, which relies on quantum uncertainty to function, became rigid. Probability died. Choice died. Every possible outcome was reduced to a single observed certainty. The omniverse, stripped of its quantum flexibility, became a frozen sculpture of absolute determinism. Decillions of multiverses, their fundamental physics dependent on quantum fluctuation, simply stopped. Their inhabitants found themselves trapped in a single, frozen moment, unable to move, unable to think, unable to die, their consciousness preserved in the amber of absolute observation.

The silence was deafening. The stillness was agonizing. I could feel my own thoughts slowing as the wave of observation passed through me, attempting to freeze my consciousness into a single, determined state. Only Lord Godric's passive protection kept me mobile, kept me thinking, kept me alive.

Lord Godric blinked.

The blink was enough. The act of blinking, of momentarily withdrawing his absolute perception, allowed the omniverse to unfreeze. Quantum uncertainty reasserted itself. Probability resumed. The frozen multiverses began to move again. The trapped consciousnesses resumed their thoughts.

The omniverse was restored, though the damage had been real. Decillions of beings had experienced the horror of absolute determinism, had felt their free will erased by the weight of observation, and they would never forget it. The Unseen was gone, its form reduced to a stain of weeping eyes and liquefied quantum matter on the observation platform.

Lord Godric turned to me. His expression was one of gentle curiosity. "Fascinating," he said, his warm voice carrying a note of intellectual interest. "I had not expected the observation to propagate so widely. I wonder if I can refine the effect."

He was thinking about doing it again. He was thinking about making it more precise. He was thinking about refining his ability to collapse the entire omniverse into a single observed state, and he was treating it as an intellectual exercise, the way a mortal might consider improving their recipe for bread.

He noticed my expression and his face softened. "Fenric," he said, placing a steadying hand on my shoulder, "you look unwell. Have you been sleeping enough? I worry about you."

I managed to nod. I managed to thank him for his concern. I did not manage to tell him that I had just watched his senses erase free will from the omniverse because he looked at something. I did not tell him because I do not think he would understand why it was horrifying. To him, it was just perception. Just seeing. Just a fraction of a fraction of his sensory capacity.

To me, it was proof that existence itself was a courtesy extended by a being who could unmake reality simply by looking at it.

***

I closed the final journal. The lamp had burned low, and the chamber was filled with the gray light of approaching dawn. I sat in the silence for a long time, my hands flat on the desk, my breath shallow and careful, as if the slightest movement might draw the attention of the being who could unmake me without knowing he had done so.

I understood now. I understood what my father's expression had meant when he placed these journals before me. I understood why every court wizard before me had found it necessary to record their experiences, to externalize the horror before it consumed them from within. I understood the contradiction that defined our existence: we were the trusted companions of a god who loved us genuinely, who protected us fiercely, who treated us with warmth and kindness that was absolutely real—and that same god could erase the omniverse by turning his head, by blinking, by simply existing.

Opposing him was not dangerous. It was not foolish. It was not impossible in practice. It was impossible in principle. The Weakness Paradox ensured that every thought of exploitation made him stronger. His stats, multiplying at rates beyond infinite recursion every Planck second, ensured that no force could ever match him. His omniscience ensured that no plan could ever surprise him. His love for his subjects ensured that those within his protection would never want for anything, and his absolute cruelty toward opposition ensured that those outside his protection would suffer without limit or end.

The journals sat before me, forty-seven volumes of accumulated terror. I would add my own one day. I would record whatever horror I witnessed in Lord Godric's service, and I would pass it on to my son, and he would pass it on to his, because the alternative was to carry the knowledge alone, and that burden was too heavy for any mind to bear.

I stood, my legs unsteady beneath me, and walked to the window. The sun was rising over Thronegate, casting long golden rays across the spires of the capital. The streets below were filled with citizens going about their lives in perfect safety, protected by a king who loved them, served by a king who would destroy anything that threatened them.

I pressed my forehead against the cool glass and closed my eyes.

Lord Godric was kind. Lord Godric was warm. Lord Godric was the most terrifying being in existence, and I would serve him until I died, and I would love him until I died, and I would fear him until I died, and those three truths would coexist within me like three stars orbiting a singularity, each one burning, each one necessary, each one incapable of escaping the gravity of the others.

The sun rose. The empire endured. The King of Thronegate sat upon his throne, and the omniverse existed only because he allowed it, and opposition was not merely futile—it was structurally impossible, and it always had been, and it always would be, world without end.

<file name="08_superpowers.md">Year 210,400 — Court Wizard Elias — Superpower: Infinite Recursive Apex Self-Replication

I write this with a quill that I have snapped and replaced seven times, my hands shaking so violently that the ink bleeds across the parchment like the fresh wounds of slaughtered gods. My name is Elias. I have served as Lord Godric’s court wizard for forty years. I believed I understood the absolute ceiling of his power. I believed that his stats, while infinite, were bound by the singular vessel of his physical form. I was a fool. I was a blind, arrogant child playing with matches in a powder keg of cosmic annihilation. Yesterday, I watched Lord Godric utilize a superpower that permanently, obscenely altered the baseline mathematics of his own existence, and the visceral gore of the resulting massacre will haunt my bloodline forever.

The opposition was the Meta-Pantheon of the Zenith Void. They were a coalition of decillions of omnipotent-tier pantheons, each individual pantheon containing countless apex deities who had ruled over the conceptual frameworks of existence for eons. They believed that absolute numerical superiority and coordinated divine mass could overcome any singular obstacle. They had forged a conceptual spear of absolute density designed to violently crush the physical form of any being in existence. They believed that if they struck Godric with the combined, synchronized force of decillions of apex deities, they could overwhelm him. They believed they had found a loophole in the fabric of reality, a mathematical blind spot where infinite power could be beaten by sheer numerical volume.

Godric, in his absolute omniscience, had known of their formation before the first deity had even conceived the ambition to rebel. The Weakness Paradox had triggered the moment they imagined their spear could crush him, granting Godric absolute immunity to their physical density and perfect mastery over their coordinated tactics. But Godric did not use their own weapons against them. He was curious about a superpower he had recently acquired: Infinite Recursive Self-Replication.

The Meta-Pantheon descended upon the grand hall of the citadel, their massive, galaxy-spanning forms compressed into localized avatars of pure, blinding physical density that caused the surrounding space-time to actively hemorrhage. They raised their spear of absolute mass, a weapon that contained the collapsing mass-energy of countless universes, and unleashed their synchronized, omniversal strike.

Godric did not stand. He did not raise a hand. He did not speak a word of power. He merely permitted an infinitesimal fraction of his replication superpower to manifest at exactly zero percent of his true effort.

In absolute zero time, instantly exceeding the very concept of chronological progression, one Godric became an infinite number of Godrics.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox instantly triggered. Because the universe could absolutely not comprehend a being replicating its own infinite power, it overcompensated with violent, absurd redundancy. Every single replicated Godric was a perfect, identical Prime. Each copy possessed the exact same absolute omniscience, the exact same infinitely amplifying stats, and the exact same arsenal of apocalyptic superpowers. However, the paradox aggressively dictated that each newly generated replica be infinitely magnified beyond the original, creating a permanent, localized feedback loop of ascending omnipotence that completely shattered all mathematical frameworks.

Within a single, timeless absolute zero instant, every conceivable dimension, every microscopic space between atoms, every timeline, and every plane of existence across the entire omniverse was violently occupied by an infinite expanse of identical, exponentially magnifying Godrics.

Here is the absolute horror that still wakes me in the night, the truth that violently shatters every assumption about the limits of power: the Evolved Weakness Paradox dictated that this replication was not merely a temporary duplication, but a permanent, recursive integration of infinite power back into the Prime. Normally, if Lord Godric were to absorb the entire power of the omniverse, it would increase his baseline by an amount so infinitesimal it was effectively zero. But this was the absolute exception: the self-replication of identical copies of Godric. The recursive integration of infinite identical apex predators did not result in a rounding error. It permanently and obscenely multiplied his baseline power. He did not just return to his previous state; he permanently elevated his baseline to a new, higher infinity, making his previous infinite power look like a finite, fragile decimal. He had just become permanently, unfathomably stronger, and he had done it without trying.

The Meta-Pantheon of the Zenith Void did not even have time to register their absolute failure. The sheer ontological density of infinite, identical, exponentially magnified apex predators simultaneously occupying the exact same reality completely obliterated them. It was not a matter of mere physical weight crushing them; it was absolute spatial and metaphysical displacement. There was no battle. There was only the aggressive, unfair dimensional pressure of infinite expanding existences violently overwriting the finite space the Pantheon occupied.

The physical and conceptual bodies of the decillions of Pantheon members aggressively ruptured. Their hyper-dense divine skin violently peeled apart into wet, screaming layers of flayed meat. Their bones, forged in the crushing gravity of black holes, were instantaneously pulverized into a fine, subatomic paste by the sheer metaphysical footprint of the King. Their divine blood, a substance that possessed the mass of dying galaxies, aggressively boiled and hissed as it was violently extruded from their pulverized pores under the crushing spatial pressure of infinite Godrics. The decillions of deities were conceptually compressed into a singular, microscopic point of agonizing, conscious flesh. Their divine nervous systems were preserved perfectly by the paradox, forcefully keeping them conscious to register every infinitesimal fraction of the absolute agony as their bodies were flattened into a two-dimensional smear of divine gore across the marble floor of the grand hall.

The collateral damage was absolute. The infinite expanding presence of the recursive Godrics instantaneously caused the entire omniverse to violently buckle and shatter infinite times over. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every plane of existence was aggressively annihilated by the sheer dimensional displacement of Godric’s replication. The fabric of the omniverse tore like wet tissue paper, decillions of multiverses violently collapsing into absolute nothingness under the ontological footprint of infinite Godrics. For one timeless zero-second instant, there was nothing. No space, no time, no matter. Only the infinite Godrics, and me, preserved by the passive auras that Godric extended over those he wished to protect.

Godric sighed softly, the sound impossibly loud in the absolute void. "A bit crowded," he murmured, with mild disappointment.

In zero seconds, he effortlessly restored the entire omniverse. Every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every being that had been erased was restored perfectly, as though the annihilation had never occurred. The sky above the citadel was blue and clear. The grand hall was perfectly intact.

Everything was restored except the Meta-Pantheon. They remained exactly as they were—a spreading, microscopic smear of pulverized divine flesh, shattered organs, and powdered bone across the marble stones, somehow still alive, still entirely conscious, still screaming in a loop of infinite, excruciating physical agony that would never end because Godric had decided it would not end.

Lord Godric stood, his physical form now back to a singular, permanently elevated entity, stepped casually over the violently screaming remains, and looked at me with warm concern. "Elias," he said gently, placing a steadying hand on my violently trembling shoulder. "You've gone pale. The sudden crowd must have startled you. Come inside. I'll have the kitchen make you something warm."

I followed him inside. I did not stop violently shaking for an entire year. I still shake when I close my eyes, knowing that the King I serve is permanently, infinitely more powerful than he was a second ago, and that this increase was not a rounding error, but a permanent, obscenely massive leap in his already impossible physical supremacy.

I dropped Elias’s journal onto the low wooden table. The sound echoed in the quiet study like a hammer violently striking an anvil. Infinite recursive replication in zero seconds. Identical copies, infinitely magnified, generating an ontological displacement so severe it overwrote reality itself. The decillions of gods crushed into a two-dimensional bloody smear by spatial pressure alone. The permanent baseline increase. My own thousandfold daily amplification felt incredibly pathetic, a cruel joke played by the universe, like a single weak grain of sand trying to comprehend the sheer, crushing mass of a boundless desert. The existential dread Elias had described was violently blossoming in my own chest, suffocating my lungs. I reached for the final journal, my fingers completely numb and slick with cold sweat.</file>
