content:
The year was 1,000,047. I was sixty-nine years old, and I had served as Lord Godric's court wizard for forty-seven years, and I had believed—truly, genuinely believed—that nothing could horrify me anymore. I had read the stat journals. I had read the superpower journals. I had watched him annihilate opposition with less effort than a man expends blinking. I had come to terms with the fact that my beloved uncle was the apex predator of all existence, that opposing him was structurally impossible, that his power grew with every Planck second and would never stop growing.

I had believed I understood the shape of my dread. I had believed it was complete.

I was wrong.

We were in his private study, the same room where I had asked him about loneliness all those years ago. The same fire crackled in the hearth. The same smell of old leather and that unidentifiable presence that saturated everything around him. Godric sat in his usual chair, looking exactly as he always looked—ruggedly handsome, forty-five years old, his heavily muscled frame relaxed, his salt-and-pepper beard neatly trimmed, his warm hazel eyes carrying the same paternal affection they had always carried.

He had summoned me. Not for a task, not for a consultation, but simply to talk. This was not unusual; Godric often sought my company, genuinely enjoying the conversation of his court wizard as a man enjoys the company of a beloved nephew. But something was different tonight. There was a quality to his stillness, a depth to his gaze, that suggested he had been thinking about something for a long time and had finally decided to share it.

"Kaedan," he said, his rich baritone carrying the familiar warmth that I had known since childhood. "There is something I have never told you. Something I have never told anyone, except Blake, and that was a very long time ago."

I sat forward in my chair, my amplified senses tingling with alertness. "What is it, Uncle Godric?"

He was quiet for a moment, his fingers stroking his beard in that contemplative gesture I had seen a thousand times. When he spoke again, his voice was softer, more reflective, carrying the weight of memory.

"You know that I lost everything when Olympus fell. Reina. Isabel. Hebe. Thorin. My kingdom. My world. You know that the grief transformed me, that it shaped the being I became."

I nodded. I had heard the story, though he rarely spoke of it directly.

"And you know that I awoke from the Stasis Event with absolute omniscience. I knew everything—the state of the omniverse, the history of every civilization, the composition of every atom, the trajectory of every thought. Everything."

"Yes," I said.

Godric nodded slowly. "Since the very instant I gained that omniscience, I have known the true origin of the Stasis Event. Not the story that everyone believes—not Thorin's incomplete spell, not the accidental interaction of forbidden magic and desperate circumstance. The true origin."

A cold weight settled in my stomach. "What do you mean, the true origin?"

Godric met my eyes, and I saw something in his gaze that I had never seen before: a calm, settled acceptance of a truth so fundamental that it had ceased to trouble him, but which had the power to reshape everything I thought I understood about his existence.

"Thorin's spell was a teleportation spell," Godric said. "A powerful one, yes. A forbidden one, certainly—it was exclusive to his bloodline, and he died mid-incantation. But it was still a mortal spell, cast by a mortal wizard, in a mortal kingdom, during a mortal catastrophe. It saved my life by sending me to the hidden sanctuary. That much is true."

He paused, letting the words settle. "But the Stasis Event—the halting of my aging, the removal of my biological needs, the beginning of my amplification, the genesis of my superpowers, the absolute concealment, the weakness paradox, the immune system, the passive shields, all of it—none of that was caused by Thorin's spell. A mortal spell could not have produced effects of that magnitude. The Stasis Event was not an accident. It was not a side effect of interrupted magic. It was deliberately created."

My mouth went dry. "Deliberately created? By whom?"

Godric's expression did not change. There was no drama in his voice, no revelation-style pause, just the calm, matter-of-fact delivery of a truth he had carried for a million years.

"By me," he said. "By a version of me from so far in the future that the year cannot be meaningfully written. A version of me so incomprehensibly more powerful than the being sitting before you that the comparison itself is absurd."

The room seemed to tilt. I gripped the armrests of my chair, my amplified heart hammering against my ribs. "What?"

"The Stasis Event was created by my future self," Godric repeated, his tone patient and unhurried. "He used Thorin's teleportation as the delivery point—the moment when I arrived in the sanctuary, he transformed the event into the Stasis Event. He is the reason my amplification is irrevocable. He is the reason my weakness paradox cannot be suppressed. He is the reason my absolute concealment is absolute. He is the origin point of everything that makes me what I am."

I stared at him, my mind struggling to process the implications. "But—why would your future self—"

"I do not know with certainty," Godric said. "I have speculated, of course. My omniscience gives me perfect knowledge of everything in the current omniverse, but my future self exists beyond the scope of current reality. He is me, but later—so far later that the span between now and then makes the span between the Fall of Olympus and the present moment look like a single Planck second. I can speculate about his motives, but I cannot know them the way I know everything else."

He leaned back in his chair, his gaze drifting toward the fire. "Perhaps he wanted to ensure that his own past was preserved—that the chain of events that led to his existence remained unbroken. Perhaps he understood something about the nature of existence that I have not yet reached, something that required the Stasis Event to occur exactly as it did. Perhaps he simply chose to, because he could, and because the act of choosing was itself a demonstration of his supremacy over causality."

Godric shook his head slowly. "But I want to be clear about something, Kaedan, because this is important. My future self did not cause the Fall of Olympus. He did not manipulate Thronegate into attacking. He did not orchestrate the deaths of Reina, Isabel, Hebe, or Thorin. Those were genuine tragedies, caused by genuine betrayal and genuine violence. My future self did not make them happen. He simply used the moment of their aftermath as the point at which to intervene."

His voice softened, and for a brief instant, I saw the ghost of the grief that had shaped him—the ancient, buried sorrow of a man who had watched his family die and been unable to save them. "He could have intervened earlier, perhaps. He could have saved them. But he did not. Whether because he could not without unraveling the causal chain that led to his own existence, or because he chose not to for reasons I cannot fathom—I do not know. But he did not cause their deaths. That distinction matters to me, and it should matter to you."

I sat in stunned silence, trying to absorb the magnitude of what he was telling me. The Stasis Event—the foundational event that had created the apex predator of the omniverse—had been engineered by Godric's own future self. Thorin's spell, which every record attributed as the cause, had merely been the delivery mechanism. The true architect was a being so far beyond current Godric that the comparison was meaningless.

And then another implication struck me, and the cold weight in my stomach became an iceberg.

"Wait," I said, my voice hoarse. "You said you've known this since the instant you gained omniscience. Since the moment you awoke from the Stasis Event. That means you've known for a million years that your future self created the Stasis Event. You've known that he made it irrevocable, that he designed your amplification and your weakness paradox and your absolute concealment to be permanent and absolute."

"Yes," Godric said calmly.

"Then you must have known, from the very beginning, that there was one thing you could not do. One timeline you could not alter. One event you could not change."

Godric met my eyes, and his expression was one of calm, settled acceptance. "Yes."

"You couldn't save them," I whispered. "You couldn't go back and save Reina and Isabel and Hebe and Thorin. Because that would require altering the timeline before the Stasis Event, and the Stasis Event was created by your future self, and his will is absolute, and you cannot overpower his will."

Godric was silent for a long moment. The fire crackled. The shadows shifted on the walls. And in that silence, I felt the shape of a horror so profound that it threatened to unmake everything I thought I understood about Godric, about power, about the nature of existence itself.

"I tried," Godric said quietly. "Shortly after I became King of Thronegate. I was still raw with grief, still shaped by the loss, still burning with the need to undo what had been done. I was omnipotent by any meaningful standard—I could travel to any timeline, alter any event, reshape any reality. The entire omniverse bent to my will. I believed that there was nothing I could not do."

He paused, and something flickered in his eyes—not pain, not sorrow, but the memory of a defeat so total, so absolute, that it had fundamentally restructured his understanding of his own existence.

"I was wrong."

I leaned forward, my hands white-knuckled on the armrests. "What happened?"

"I attempted to travel back to before the Stasis Event. To Olympus. To the day of Isabel's wedding. I was going to warn them. I was going to save them. I had the power, I had the knowledge, I had the will—and I was stopped."

"Stopped? By what?"

Godric's expression was calm, almost peaceful. "By the shadow of the afterthought of my future self."

The words hung in the air like a blade suspended above my neck.

"Not my future self himself," Godric continued, his voice measured and unhurried. "Not his actual presence, not his conscious attention, not his deliberate intervention. A shadow. An afterthought. The faintest, most infinitesimal echo of his will, bleeding backward through time from a point so distant that the year cannot be written. A residual impression of his existence, so faint that he was almost certainly not aware of it."

He looked at me, and his calm was more terrifying than any display of rage could have been. "And that shadow—that afterthought, that faintest echo of his will—completely and effortlessly defeated my attempt to alter the pre-Stasis timeline. Not through conflict. Not through struggle. Not through a battle of wills that I narrowly lost. I was defeated absolutely, totally, and instantaneously. My attempt was erased before it could fully form. My intention was rendered impossible before it could be executed. I was not merely overpowered—I was rendered irrelevant. The shadow of my future self's afterthought treated my full, desperate, grief-driven assault on the timeline the way you might treat a speck of dust that had landed on your sleeve. It was not a contest. It was not even a recognition. I simply... failed. Completely. Effortlessly. Absolutely."

I could not breathe. The image was forming in my mind, and it was the most horrifying thing I had ever contemplated.

Godric—the Godric I knew, the Godric who could erase the omniverse without noticing, the Godric whose stats multiplied beyond infinite recursion every Planck second, the Godric whose weakest passive aura could annihilate the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed to a fraction of a fraction of its power—this Godric, at the height of his fresh, grief-driven, fully unleashed power, had been casually, effortlessly swatted aside by the faintest echo of a being so incomprehensibly more powerful that the comparison between them was meaningless.

Not by the future Godric himself. By the shadow of his afterthought.

"Think about what that means, Kaedan," Godric said softly, and his voice carried no bitterness, no resentment, no lingering grief—only calm, settled acceptance. "You know how powerful I am. You have read the journals. You have seen what I can do at effectively zero percent of my true capacity. You have struggled to comprehend the gap between me and the rest of the omniverse—a gap so vast that the combined power of every other entity in existence is less than a rounding error compared to my baseline."

He leaned forward, his warm eyes meeting mine. "Now try to imagine a being so far beyond me that my full power—my complete, unrestricted, unlimited, infinite power—is less than a rounding error compared to his baseline. Try to imagine a being for whom I am what a mortal is to me—a speck of dust, a fleeting thought, a negligible quantity. And then understand that even that comparison is inadequate, because the gap between me and my future self is not merely vast—it is a category error. It is like comparing the concept of nothing to the concept of everything and finding that even the comparison gives nothing too much credit."

I was shaking. My amplified body, which could withstand the crushing gravity of neutron stars, was trembling like a leaf in a storm. "Uncle Godric, that's—"

"I know," he said gently. "It is difficult to process. But I need you to understand it, because this is the truth of my existence. This is the one thing I cannot do—the one limitation on my otherwise absolute power. I cannot alter the timeline before the Stasis Event. I cannot save Olympus. I cannot bring back Reina, or Isabel, or Hebe, or Thorin. Not because I lack the power, but because the source of my power—my future self—has decreed that the pre-Stasis past is sealed. And his will is so far beyond mine that the question of overpowering it does not even arise."

He sat back, and a small, almost amused smile crossed his features. "You would think this would trouble me. You would think that the knowledge of this one limitation—that there is one thing I cannot do, one timeline I cannot touch, one loss I cannot undo—would be a source of endless grief or frustration. And for a time, it was. In the years immediately after my awakening, the inability to save them was a wound that would not close. I raged against it. I threw myself against the barrier again and again, each time being effortlessly rebuffed by a will so absolute that my resistance was not even registered."

He paused, and his expression softened into something approaching contentment. "But that was a long time ago, Kaedan. A very long time. And over the millennia, I have come to understand something about this limitation that I initially failed to grasp."

I stared at him, my throat tight. "What?"

"This limitation is not truly a weakness," Godric said, and his voice carried the patient clarity of a teacher explaining a concept to a student who was struggling to keep up. "It appears to be one, certainly. It is the one thing I cannot do—the one timeline I cannot alter, the one loss I cannot undo. But consider what the limitation actually represents."

He held up his hand, ticking off points on his fingers with the casual precision of a man who had spent a million years thinking about this. "First: the limitation exists because my future self is incomprehensibly more powerful than I am. This means that my power is not static—it is growing toward a future state of being so far beyond my current state that the gap between us cannot be meaningfully described. My future is not uncertain. My future is not in jeopardy. My future is guaranteed, because the being I will become has already demonstrated that his power exceeds mine by a margin that renders the comparison absurd."

He held up a second finger. "Second: because my future self created the Stasis Event, and because the Stasis Event is the source of all my power mechanics—my amplification, my superpower genesis, my absolute concealment, my weakness paradox, my immune system, my passive shields, my sentient auras—all of these systems are anchored by a will so absolute that no external force can override, suppress, deny, or exploit them. No enemy can turn my own power against me, because the origin of that power is a being so incomprehensibly more powerful than anything else in existence that the very concept of exploitation becomes meaningless. My systems are not merely robust—they are guaranteed by the future Godric's absolute supremacy."

A third finger. "Third: this means that my one apparent limitation is actually a reinforcement of my absolute invincibility. The inability to alter the pre-Stasis past is not a vulnerability that can be exploited—it is a demonstration of the fact that my power is backed by a source so overwhelmingly supreme that even I cannot override it. And since that source is my own future self, the limitation does not represent an external threat—it represents the absolute guarantee that my power will continue to grow, that my future is secure, and that nothing in existence can ever meaningfully challenge the systems that make me what I am."

He lowered his hand and looked at me with that calm, settled acceptance that I now recognized as the peace of a being who had fully comprehended the nature of his own existence. "So you see, Kaedan, my one true weakness is not truly a weakness at all. It is a strength. The most absolute strength imaginable. It is the guarantee that I will never be defeated, that my power will never be exploited, that my future is not merely probable but certain, and that the being I will become is so far beyond the being I am now that even the word 'beyond' is insufficient."

He smiled, and the smile was warm and genuine and utterly at peace. "I am no longer troubled by this limitation. I have accepted it. I have even come to find it amusing—this one thing I cannot do, this one timeline I cannot touch, and the reason I cannot do it is because my own future is so obscenely, incomprehensibly powerful that his afterthought was sufficient to absolutely crush my best effort. How can I be troubled by that? How can I be grieved by the knowledge that my future self is so vast, so supreme, so absolute that even his shadow is beyond my capacity to challenge? It is not a source of sorrow. It is a source of satisfaction. It is confirmation that the trajectory of my existence is toward a state of being so perfect, so complete, so overwhelmingly supreme, that the journey itself is the most satisfying experience imaginable."

He reached for his tea and took a slow, contemplative sip. "Does that make sense to you, Kaedan?"

I sat in my chair, my body rigid, my mind reeling, my soul crumbling under the weight of what I had just heard.

He had tried to save them. The omnipotent Godric, fresh with grief and blazing with power, had tried to travel back and save his family, and he had been swatted aside like an insect by the faintest echo of his own future self's will. Not by the future Godric himself—by the shadow of an afterthought. And that shadow had been so absolute, so effortless, so total in its dominance that the attempt had been erased before it could fully form.

And he was not troubled by this. He was amused. He was satisfied. He had accepted the absolute impossibility of saving his family and had found in that impossibility a source of contentment, because the impossibility was proof that his future was guaranteed to be even more incomprehensibly powerful than his present.

I tried to grasp the scale of what this meant. The current Godric—the Godric sitting across from me, sipping tea with warm, paternal affection—was already so powerful that the rest of the omniverse combined was less than a rounding error compared to him. His stats multiplied beyond infinite recursion every Planck second. His weakest passive aura could annihilate the strongest coalition in existence while nerfed to a fraction that could not be measured. His immune system treated the possibility of harm as an object to be hunted and erased before it existed. His weakness paradox made every perceived exploit self-defeating before it could be formulated. His absolute concealment was so perfect that the omniverse could survive his presence.

And his future self was so far beyond this that the current Godric's full, unrestricted, unlimited power was less than nothing in comparison.

The shadow of that future self's afterthought—just the shadow, just the afterthought, just the faintest echo of a will that was almost certainly not consciously aware of the interference—had absolutely and effortlessly crushed the desperate, grief-driven assault of a being who could erase the omniverse without noticing.

What did that say about the future Godric? What did that say about the trajectory of Godric's existence? What did that say about the nature of power itself, when the most powerful being in the current omniverse was still so far beneath his own future self that the comparison was a category error?

And what did it say about me—about every entity in existence, about every civilization, about every god and every coalition and every desperate, struggling being who tried to survive in a universe that was fundamentally designed to feed a being whose power was guaranteed by a future so absolute that even the concept of challenge was meaningless?

I opened my mouth to speak, but no words came. My amplified mind, which could process calculations that would shatter mortal brains, could not formulate a response to what I had just heard. The horror was not in the revelation itself—the horror was in the implications.

Godric's one weakness was not a weakness. It was the most absolute guarantee of invincibility imaginable. The inability to alter the pre-Stasis past was not a vulnerability—it was proof that his power was anchored by a source so supreme that no external force could ever override, suppress, or exploit any of his systems. His amplification could not be stopped, because the being who created it was beyond challenge. His weakness paradox could not be circumvented, because the being who originated it was beyond exploitation. His absolute concealment could not be pierced, because the being who designed it was beyond comprehension. Every system that made Godric what he was—every mechanism of his godlike supremacy—was guaranteed by the future Godric's absolute, incomprehensible, overwhelming supremacy.

And that supremacy was growing. Every Planck second, the gap between current Godric and future Godric widened, because current Godric was constantly becoming more powerful, which meant future Godric was constantly becoming even more incomprehensibly powerful, which meant the guarantee of invincibility was constantly becoming more absolute, which meant the impossibility of opposing him was constantly becoming more impossible.

It was not a weakness. It was an engine of perpetual, self-reinforcing, guaranteed ascension. And the fuel for that engine was the absolute certainty that the being Godric would become was so far beyond any possible challenge that the concept of challenge itself would cease to have meaning.

I stared at Godric, at my beloved uncle, at the warm, kind, paternal figure who had held me as a child and taught me magic and attended my coming-of-age ceremony, and I saw something I had never seen before. I saw a being who was not merely powerful, but whose power was structurally, fundamentally, ontologically guaranteed by the absolute supremacy of his own future. I saw a being whose every system—every stat, every superpower, every defense, every mechanism of his godlike existence—was backed by a source so incomprehensibly supreme that the question of exploitation did not even arise.

I saw a being who could never be defeated, not because he was merely stronger than everything else, but because the origin of his power was a future version of himself so overwhelmingly omnipotent that even his afterthought was sufficient to absolutely crush any attempt to undermine the systems that made him invincible.

I saw a being whose one apparent limitation was actually the most absolute guarantee of his own invincibility.

And I saw a being who was perfectly, genuinely, contentedly at peace with all of this.

"You're not afraid," I said, and my voice was barely a whisper. "You're not troubled. You're not grieving. You've accepted it."

Godric nodded, and his expression was one of gentle, patient understanding—the expression of a teacher whose student was finally beginning to grasp a difficult concept. "I accepted it a long time ago, Kaedan. The grief was real, and it shaped me, and I will never forget what I lost. But the inability to undo that loss is not a source of ongoing suffering. It is a fact of my existence, as fundamental as the amplification itself, and I have made my peace with it."

He set down his tea cup and leaned forward, his warm eyes meeting mine with an intensity that made my chest ache. "I know this is difficult to hear. I know it challenges everything you thought you understood about my existence. But I wanted you to know the truth—not because it changes anything, but because you are my court wizard, and Blake's descendant, and the closest thing to a son I will ever have. You deserved to know."

I sat in the chair, my body still, my mind churning, my soul cracking under the weight of implications that seemed to expand with every passing moment. The truth was not merely horrifying—it was foundational. It restructured everything. It meant that Godric's power was not merely overwhelming but guaranteed. It meant that his invincibility was not merely practical but absolute. It meant that the impossibility of opposing him was not merely a fact of existence but a structural feature of reality itself, backed by a future being so supreme that the concept of opposition was rendered meaningless before it could even form.

And it meant something else—something that struck at the very core of my understanding of Godric as a person, as a being, as the uncle I loved.

He had tried to save them. He had tried to go back and save Reina and Isabel and Hebe and Thorin, and he had failed, not because he lacked the power, but because his own future had decreed that the past was sealed. And he had accepted this. He had made his peace with it. He had found in the impossibility a source of satisfaction, because the impossibility was proof that his future was even more omnipotent than his present.

The grief had been real. The loss had been genuine. But the being who had experienced that grief and that loss had grown so powerful, so far beyond the reach of mortal emotion, that the grief had become... what? A memory? A data point? A historical fact that no longer carried emotional weight?

I looked at his warm, kind eyes, and I saw the same affection I had always seen. The same paternal love. The same genuine care for my wellbeing. And I realized that these emotions were real—that Godric was not pretending to care about me, that his warmth was not a mask, that his love was genuine.

But I also realized that the same being who loved me with genuine warmth was a being whose power was guaranteed by a future so absolute that the concept of challenge was meaningless, and that this guarantee was the source of his contentment, and that this contentment was built on the foundation of a loss so profound that it had transformed a warm, loving mortal king into an apex predator of existence.

The cognitive dissonance was unbearable. The warmth and the horror coexisted inside me like two stars orbiting a singularity, each one burning, each one real, each one incapable of canceling the other.

"Uncle Godric," I said, and my voice was hollow. "Does it ever bother you that you can't save them? Not the limitation itself—I understand that. But the fact that your future self chose not to save them. He could have. He could have intervened earlier. He could have prevented the Fall of Olympus entirely. But he didn't. He used the moment of their deaths as the delivery point for the Stasis Event, and he didn't save them."

Godric was quiet for a long moment. The fire crackled. The shadows shifted. And when he spoke, his voice was thoughtful, measured, carrying the weight of a million years of contemplation.

"I have asked myself that question," he said. "Many times. And I have not found a satisfactory answer. Perhaps he could not intervene without unraveling the causal chain that led to his own existence. Perhaps he understood something about the nature of time and causality that I have not yet reached—something that made intervention impossible without consequences worse than the loss. Perhaps he chose not to for reasons I cannot fathom, reasons that will become clear to me only when I reach the point in my existence where I am the one making that choice."

He paused, and his expression was not troubled, not resentful, but simply contemplative. "Or perhaps—and this is the explanation I find most likely—he simply did not think about it the way a mortal would. He is so far beyond me, and I am so far beyond mortal emotion, that the emotional calculus of saving or not saving may not have been a factor in his decision at all. He may have created the Stasis Event for reasons entirely unrelated to the fate of Olympus—reasons that I will understand when I become him, and that I cannot comprehend now."

He shook his head slowly. "I do not know. I have speculated, and I will continue to speculate, but I will not know until I reach that point in my existence. And by then, the question may not even be meaningful anymore."

He looked at me, and his expression softened into the warm, paternal affection that I had known all my life. "Does that bother you, Kaedan?"

I stared at him, and the horror was a living thing inside my chest, coiled and crushing and absolute. It bothered me. It bothered me more than anything I had ever learned. It bothered me because it meant that the most powerful being in existence—a being whose future self was so incomprehensibly supreme that his afterthought could absolutely crush any attempt to undermine his systems—had chosen not to save his own family, and the reason for that choice was so far beyond mortal comprehension that even Godric himself could only speculate.

It bothered me because it meant that the trajectory of Godric's existence was toward a state of being so far beyond mortal emotion that the question of saving or not saving his own wife and children might not even be meaningful.

It bothered me because it meant that the warm, kind, loving uncle who sat across from me was on a path toward becoming a being for whom the loss of his family was not a wound that would not heal, but a fact that had ceased to carry emotional weight.

It bothered me because it meant that the Godric I loved—the Godric who held me as a child and taught me magic and attended my coming-of-age ceremony—was a transitional state, a waystation on the journey toward a future so absolute that the warmth I cherished would be as meaningless as a mortal's grief over a broken toy.

But I could not say any of this. I could not articulate the horror that was crushing my soul. I could only sit in my chair, looking at the warm, kind face of my beloved uncle, and feel the existential dread settle over me like a shroud.

"No," I lied. "It doesn't bother me."

Godric smiled, and the smile was warm and genuine and utterly at peace. "Good," he said. "Now finish your tea. You look like you could use something warm."

I picked up the cup. My hands were shaking. The tea was cold. I drank it anyway, and the cold spread through me, and the dread settled into my bones, and I knew that I would carry this horror for whatever years remained to me.

Godric's one weakness was not a weakness. It was the most absolute guarantee of invincibility imaginable. His power was backed by a future so supreme that even his afterthought was sufficient to absolutely crush any challenge. His systems could never be overridden, suppressed, or exploited, because the being who created them was beyond challenge. His trajectory was toward a state of being so far beyond current comprehension that the comparison was meaningless.

And the Godric I loved—the warm, kind, paternal figure who had shaped my entire existence—was content with this. He was at peace. He was satisfied. He had accepted the impossibility of saving his family and found in that impossibility a source of contentment, because the impossibility was proof that his future was even more omnipotent than his present.

I sat in the study of the King of Thronegate, drinking cold tea in the presence of a being whose one apparent limitation was actually the most absolute guarantee of his own invincibility, and I felt the existential dread settle into my soul like a stone dropped into still water, sending ripples of horror through every thought I would ever have for the rest of my life.

The fire crackled. The shadows shifted. And Godric smiled at me with warm, genuine, paternal affection, and the smile was the most terrifying thing I had ever seen.

The year 999...9 (you don't need to spell it out. Just know it's so large that it's impossible to write). King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year -10,000 (you must also indicate that godric could've effortlessly granted way more potent stasis event with more devastating effects (he can perfectly copy his current mechanism to the past Godric) with no cost to him, but decided against it to "hold back"--you must note the crazy ridiculousness of this since even what Godric considered as the “held back” version is so incomprehensibly powerful… Godric at year 0 was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of the creation combined, and Godric genuinely believed that it would preserve some kind of mortal struggle to his past self, something that is super not true. And also note the cause-effect of this: if he grants his past self a really powerful version, he himself who is in the future and has been amplified from his past self will even be more incomprehensibly powerful since the starting "base" is also already become so much stronger and thus he can grant even more powerful version to his past infinitely. Clive's mind must be even more overwhelmed at this absurd realisation.). Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his terrifying presence, his everything at this point.

All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, showing clearly how they become even more absurdly superfluously terrifying than before, to the point of the “old version of his power mechanism” which are already ridiculously powerful feeling so weak in comparison. You must keep in mind that Godric, who had been a mortal once, still believes in mortal resilience and free will, so if there are any evolved versions of his power mechanisms that alter free will they should be consciously suppressed by Godric.

When Godric just emerged from stasis, for a very long time, countless sentience still formed some opposition against him, despite Godric himself never regarded them as any real oppositions–to him, those "oppositions" were just a brief fun distraction to test his stats and powers. Since some googolplexian years ago, however, all oppositions have stopped completely: there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing Godric. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric. Note: you must not suggest that Godric alters the free will to oppose him‐-Godric genuinely believes in mortal resilience.

You must also include a few scenes where he interacts with really powerful beings around him (show him as friendly to those), who after being completely traumatized by Godric, has become his lowly palace servants, gardeners, etc.. Those really powerful beings are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of (do not say they opposed godric--Godric is unforgiving to opposition) and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Clive, his court wizard who is Blake’s descendant. Clive is 50 years old.

Key requirements:

5. Note the absurdity of this "holding back" since even the held-back version made Year 0 Godric infinitely stronger than creation combined

12. These beings didn't oppose Godric (he's unforgiving to opposition) - they became terrified through other means

- Show interactions with powerful servant beings and their backstory
